scenario: An offline-first PWA for field inspectors who lose connectivity for hours. Team debates Redux Toolkit (with RTK Query + redux-persist + offline middleware) vs Zustand (with persist middleware + custom sync queue) for managing inspection forms, photos, and pending submissions.
context: Team of 5 engineers, 8-month project, target devices are mid-range Android tablets, must sync 100+ inspections per inspector per day, conflicts must be resolvable by inspectors in the field.
alex_initial_position: Zustand. Redux Toolkit's serialization checks and immer overhead kill perf on low-end Androids. We need a slim store with explicit persistence and our own sync queue - that's 200 lines, not a framework.
sam_initial_position: Redux Toolkit. Offline-first is a solved problem with RTK Query's offline patterns + redux-persist. Rebuilding it in Zustand means we own undo/redo, optimistic updates, conflict resolution, and a query cache - that's 6 months of bugs.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: RTK Query's cache is designed for online-first with stale-while-revalidate. Offline-first inverts the assumption: writes happen locally first, sync is best-effort. RTK Query forces us to bend its mutation lifecycle to fit, and we end up with a custom middleware anyway.
debate.evidence: RTK Query docs explicitly say 'offline support is not a primary use case'; the recommended approach is a custom listenerMiddleware + manual cache hydration.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Fair, but RTK gives us the listener middleware, devtools time-travel, and serializable actions for free. When a field inspector reports 'I lost my form', we can replay actions from the persisted log. Zustand has no action log - state mutations are opaque.
debate.evidence: Redux DevTools' action replay has caught 14 of the last 20 sync bugs in our previous PWA; Zustand's middleware ecosystem has no equivalent.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: You don't need Redux to get an action log. Append-only event log is 30 lines. The cost of Redux is paid on every render: useSelector with shallow eq runs on every dispatch, even for unrelated state. On a Snapdragon 680 that's measurable.
debate.evidence: I profiled a 50-field form: RTK with useSelector per field = 18ms render; same form with Zustand selectors = 6ms. The diff is selector enumeration cost in Redux's subscription model.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Use reselect or RTK's createSelector with proper memoization and that drops to 8ms. But fine, perf is closer than I claimed. My deeper concern: conflict resolution. When inspector A and inspector B edit the same record and reconnect, we need a merge UI. Redux's normalized cache (createEntityAdapter) makes this trivial; Zustand makes you hand-roll it.
debate.evidence: createEntityAdapter gives us upsertMany, updateMany with conflict detectors via prepareCallback. In our previous Zustand offline app, we wrote 400 lines of merge logic that's still buggy.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Conflict resolution is domain-specific - inspector apps need 'last writer wins by timestamp' OR 'manual merge for safety-critical fields'. createEntityAdapter doesn't help with the UX; it's just a normalized map. We need a domain layer either way, and Zustand keeps it explicit.
debate.evidence: Our safety-critical fields (defect severity, location) require manual review - no library can automate that. The 'merge UI' is 80% of the work and is framework-independent.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: OK, conflict UI is framework-agnostic. But here's where RTK wins: photo uploads. RTK Query's mutation lifecycle with onQueryStarted lets us optimistically add the photo to the inspection, then roll back on failure. The cache stays consistent. With Zustand we write that lifecycle manually for every mutation.
debate.evidence: RTK Query's optimistic update pattern: 8 lines per mutation. Equivalent Zustand: 35 lines (queue, rollback, dedup) per mutation, times 12 mutations in our app.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: That's a real point. But we can abstract: one `createOptimisticAction(name, mutator, rollback)` helper in Zustand. 60 lines once, then 8 lines per mutation - same as RTK Query. And the helper is debuggable in our code, not buried in node_modules.
debate.evidence: I wrote this helper for a previous project: 58 lines including types, handles rollback, retry, and dedup. Used across 23 mutations.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: I'll concede the helper approach works. My final argument: team velocity. Three of our five engineers know Redux deeply from prior jobs. Onboarding to RTK Query patterns is a half-day; onboarding to our custom Zustand-based offline framework is a week per engineer plus tribal knowledge.
debate.evidence: State of JS 2024: Redux still #1 by usage among teams >5 engineers. Hiring market has 10x more Redux-experienced devs than Zustand-experienced.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Hiring is a real constraint. I'll meet you halfway: RTK for the global state and entity normalization, but skip RTK Query - use a thin custom sync layer that dispatches RTK actions. Best of both: framework benefits for storage, domain control for sync.
debate.evidence: This hybrid is what Linear's offline architecture does per their 2023 engineering blog: Redux-like store, custom sync layer on top.

where_they_agree: Conflict resolution is domain logic, not framework logic
Optimistic updates need a reusable abstraction either way
Persisted action log is valuable for debugging field issues
The hiring market favors Redux familiarity
where_they_disagree: Whether RTK Query's online-first design fits offline-first usage
How much render perf matters on mid-range Android
Whether normalized cache helpers (createEntityAdapter) provide enough value to justify framework weight
context_dependent_answer: Choose Redux Toolkit (without RTK Query) + custom sync layer when (a) team has Redux experience, (b) you need action-log debugging for field issues, or (c) you use createEntityAdapter for normalized entities. Choose Zustand when (a) team is small (<4) and senior, (b) bundle size matters for cold-start on low-end devices, or (c) you want a single source of truth without framework opinions. Avoid RTK Query for offline-first - its mutation lifecycle assumes online-first.

strengths: Explicit sync queue, easy to debug
weaknesses: No action log for time-travel debugging
Normalized entity helpers must be written by hand
Serialization checks slow on low-end Android (can disable)

decision_framework: debate-076: judge the state management architecture debate in this scenario: An offline-first PWA for field inspectors who lose connectivity for hours. Team debates Redux Toolkit (with RTK Query + redux-persist + offline middleware) vs Zustand (with persist. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors pick the library based on Twitter sentiment or bundle size badges. Seniors recognize that offline-first apps are 80% domain logic (conflict resolution, sync queue, retry policy) and 20% framework. The framework choice barely moves the needle; what matters is whether the team can debug a field issue at 2am six months from now. Choose the tool with the best observability story for YOUR team, not the smallest bundle.

scenario: A design system team is building a ComboBox/Select component used across 40+ product surfaces. Debate is between compound components (Select.Root, Select.Trigger, Select.Options, Select.Option) vs prop-based API (<Select options={[]} onChange={} renderOption={} />).
context: Design system used by 80 engineers across 6 product teams, currently shipping v2 (prop-based) with 47 props and growing. Migration cost to compound is ~3 weeks. New use cases (async loading, multi-select, grouping) keep adding props.
alex_initial_position: Stay prop-based. Compound APIs look elegant in demos but devs end up copy-pasting 12 lines for every Select. Multiply by 200 Selects in the app and that's noise. A well-designed prop API is more concise and easier to lint.
sam_initial_position: Migrate to compound. The 47-prop signature is screaming at us - we've reached the prop-soup ceiling. New use cases keep birthing new props (renderOption, renderEmpty, renderGroupHeader, optionDisabled, optionDescription) and the API surface is unlearnable.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Look at the current API: we have onChange, onSelectionChange, onValueChange (legacy), onBlur, onMenuOpenChange, plus 8 render props. New devs spend 30 minutes reading docs for a Select. Compound APIs expose primitives - devs assemble what they need.
debate.evidence: Our internal docs analytics: Select.tsx docs page has 12-minute median time on page. Radix Select (compound) has 3-minute median per public usage stats.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Time-on-docs measures discovery, not productivity. A senior writes a Select with our prop API in 30 seconds because IntelliSense surfaces the props. With compound they have to remember which sub-components exist and how they nest. That's tribal knowledge.
debate.evidence: In a Stripe internal study (cited in their design system talk), compound APIs increased PR review cycles by 1.4x because reviewers had to verify nesting was correct.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: IntelliSense works for compound too - Select.Root types Select.Trigger as its child via slot-based typing. And nesting isn't tribal - it's syntactically enforceable: Select.Option must be inside Select.Options must be inside Select.Root, or TS errors.
debate.evidence: Radix and Ariakit both enforce parent-child contracts via React Context + type guards; misuse fails at the type level in TS strict mode.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Type-enforced nesting still requires devs to write the nesting. 12 lines vs 1 line. For a Select that appears 50 times in a form-heavy app, that's 550 extra lines of boilerplate to maintain. Refactoring all of them when the design changes is a nightmare.
debate.evidence: GitHub's primer-react went compound and then added <ActionMenu> as a 'simplified' wrapper because the compound API was too verbose for common cases. They now maintain both.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: That's the answer: ship BOTH. A compound primitive (SelectPrimitive.*) for the 5% of cases that need full control, and a high-level Select that wraps it for the 95% common case. shadcn/ui does exactly this - they generate compound code via CLI but it reads like a high-level component.
debate.evidence: shadcn/ui's <Select> component is ~80 lines of pre-composed Radix primitives. Consumers import one <Select> but customizers can edit the generated code.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: OK, the two-layer approach is reasonable. But who maintains the high-level wrapper when the design changes? If every product team copies the wrapper (shadcn-style), we lose centralized control. If we publish the wrapper as part of the design system, we're back to the prop-soup problem in the wrapper.
debate.evidence: Our brand refresh in Q2 required updating Select styling. Centralized prop-based Select: one PR, deploy, done. Decentralized copy-paste model: 6 product teams to coordinate.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Publish the wrapper centrally, but keep it deliberately limited - 8 props max. Everything beyond requires dropping to primitives. This forces a design conversation: 'why does your Select need a custom group header?' Maybe the design is wrong.
debate.evidence: Vercel's geist-ui v2 caps wrapper props at 10. Their internal data: 92% of Select usages use the wrapper, 8% drop to primitives, average dev time per Select dropped 30%.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: I can live with that. Cap the wrapper at 8 props, expose primitives for escapes, and ban prop-creep via lint rule: 'if you need a new prop on Select wrapper, file a design RFC.' That keeps the API stable and gives an out for edge cases.
debate.evidence: Cap+escape pattern is what Material-UI v6 adopted; their prop count on core components dropped 40% with no functional regression.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Agreed. The migration plan: build SelectPrimitive.* (compound, type-safe context) first, then build Select (wrapper, capped props) on top. Migrate existing usages to wrapper via codemod. Edge cases get primitives.
debate.evidence: This is exactly Radix's positioning - primitives + shadcn wrappers - now adopted by Linear, Vercel, and Supabase.

where_they_agree: A two-layer approach (primitives + wrapper) is the right pattern
Wrapper props must be capped to prevent regression
where_they_disagree: Whether boilerplate cost of compound is significant in form-heavy apps
Whether decentralized customization (shadcn model) or centralized wrapper is better
How much weight to give IntelliSense vs type-enforced nesting
context_dependent_answer: Choose compound primitives + capped wrapper when (a) the design system serves 5+ teams with divergent needs, (b) you've already hit prop-soup, or (c) you need to expose escape hatches without breaking the common case. Choose prop-only API when (a) the team is small (<3 product surfaces), (b) the component is stable (no new variants in 6+ months), or (c) consistency matters more than flexibility (regulated industries).

strengths: Edge case: drop to primitives, full control
weaknesses: Two APIs to document and maintain
Edge cases force devs to learn both layers
Wrapper customization (e.g. custom option renderer) requires escape to primitives

strengths: One import, no nesting to remember
Single PR to change styling globally
weaknesses: Render-prop signatures hard to type generically
Escape hatches require new props (impossible to anticipate all cases)

decision_framework: debate-077: judge the component composition patterns debate in this scenario: A design system team is building a ComboBox/Select component used across 40+ product surfaces. Debate is between compound components (Select.Root, Select.Trigger, Select.Options, S. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors think compound vs prop-based is a stylistic preference. Seniors recognize it's a question of who absorbs complexity: the consumer (compound = more code per use) or the maintainer (prop-based = more props to support). The right answer is usually 'both, layered' - primitives for power users, wrappers for the common case. They also miss that prop-soup is a leading indicator of an API that's outgrown its model; it doesn't get better with one more prop.

scenario: A survey builder tool lets users create surveys with conditional logic (show question N only if question 3 = 'yes'). The frontend renders the survey using either React Hook Form or final-form. The question: which handles conditional fields better?
context: 5 engineers, 100+ surveys/month authored, each survey has 5-50 questions with up to 4 levels of nested conditionals. Form state must serialize for partial response saving.
alex_initial_position: React Hook Form with watch + conditional render. Uncontrolled, fast, and the conditional logic is just JSX conditionals based on watched values. final-form's subscription model is more granular but the overhead isn't worth it for survey-scale forms (<100 fields).
sam_initial_position: final-form with subscription-based fields. Conditional logic in RHF requires `useWatch` which re-renders the parent on every keystroke of watched fields. With 4-level nested conditionals, parents re-render constantly. final-form's per-field subscriptions handle this naturally.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: useWatch only re-renders when the watched value changes (not on every keystroke unless you watch the actual input). For survey questions with discrete answers (radio, select), the watched value changes once per click. Parent re-renders are infrequent and cheap.
debate.evidence: useWatch on a radio group fires once per selection, not per keystroke. Profiled at <0.5ms per re-render.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For radio/select that's fine. For text input questions with conditional follow-ups ('if answer mentions X show Y') you'd need to watch the text input on every keystroke, which does cause parent re-renders. final-form subscriptions are per-field, so the conditional-displaying parent only re-renders when its specific subscribed value changes.
debate.evidence: final-form subscription model: each field subscribes to {value, error, touched}; parent only re-renders on subscribed-changes.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Text-input conditional follow-ups are rare in survey patterns. Most conditionals are 'if answer was option X show this'. For the text-input case, debounce the watch with useDeferredValue or a custom debounced watch.
debate.evidence: useDeferredValue defers the watch update until the input has idled.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: useDeferredValue is a workaround. final-form solves it natively. For a tool authoring 100 surveys/month with arbitrary conditional logic, you want the form library to handle the case naturally, not workarounds per case.
debate.evidence: Tool velocity dictates picking the library that matches the worst-case naturally.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: final-form is in much more limited maintenance than RHF. RHF has 40K+ stars, frequent releases, large community. final-form is solid but the bus factor concerns me for production tooling.
debate.evidence: Maintenance velocity for RHF vs final-form: substantial gap.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: True. For long-term bet, RHF wins. But for THIS specific app shape (deep conditionals, text-input conditionals), final-form's per-field subscriptions are technically better. Trade-off: better ergonomics vs better community.
debate.evidence: Local optimum (final-form) vs global optimum (RHF + workarounds).

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Pick RHF + custom subscription wrapper for the text-input conditional case. Build a thin `useWatchDebounced` hook. 30 lines of code. Get RHF's community + final-form's perf for the niche case.
debate.evidence: Custom hooks bridge gaps.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Reasonable. RHF + useWatchDebounced for text-input conditionals + native RHF for discrete conditionals. Document the pattern.
debate.evidence: Layered solution.

where_they_agree: Discrete conditionals (radio/select) are fine in RHF
Community/maintenance velocity matters for long-term tools
Custom subscription wrappers can bridge gaps
where_they_disagree: Whether final-form's per-field model is worth its smaller ecosystem
How common text-input conditionals really are in survey patterns
context_dependent_answer: Survey builder with discrete conditionals: RHF. Survey builder with frequent text-input conditionals: RHF + custom debounced watch. Form builder with arbitrary user-defined conditionals (like a low-code tool): final-form might win if you can absorb the ecosystem risk. Single survey app (1-2 surveys): native React state.
decision_framework: debate-078: judge the form library choices debate in this scenario: A survey builder tool lets users create surveys with conditional logic (show question N only if question 3 = 'yes'). The frontend renders the survey using either React Hook Form or. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors pick by GitHub stars. Senior insight: form library choice is downstream of conditional complexity. Most forms are flat - any library works. Deeply nested conditionals expose the subscription model differences. Profile the worst-case scenario, not the happy path. Also: survey builders are not typical forms - they author forms, which is a meta-form problem with different requirements.

scenario: A dashboard refreshes 30 widgets when a global filter changes. Options: stale-while-revalidate with SWR keeping stale data visible during refetch, vs React Query with manual placeholderData management, vs imperative invalidation across all widgets.
context: Filter changes ~10 times/minute during heavy use. Stale data shown during 200-500ms refetch is acceptable. 30 widgets × per-widget loading skeletons = visual chaos.
alex_initial_position: SWR. Its name is its philosophy - stale data is shown, revalidation happens in background, UI doesn't flash. Built for this exact pattern. React Query requires placeholderData configuration per query; SWR does it by default.
sam_initial_position: React Query with `placeholderData: (prev) => prev`. The default is opinionated towards 'show stale during refetch' once you add that one option. React Query has better TypeScript inference, better devtools, more mature mutation patterns. Don't pick a library based on a default that's a single config away.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: React Query's mutation patterns are great for mutations. For pure reads, SWR is simpler. Less config, fewer concepts. For a team that doesn't have query-management experience, SWR's smaller API surface is faster to onboard.
debate.evidence: SWR docs are ~half the length of React Query docs. Onboarding correlates.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Onboarding cost is real but one-time. Maintenance cost is forever. React Query's devtools (query inspector, cache visualization) save hours of debugging on every cache-related bug. SWR's devtools are minimal.
debate.evidence: React Query DevTools panel shows cache state, refetch status, dependencies - critical for debugging 'why isn't this widget updating' bugs.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Devtools is a strong point. For a dashboard with 30 widgets, the 'why isn't this updating' debugging will happen. Conceding that React Query's devtools matter.
debate.evidence: Production debugging tool quality is leverage.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Plus React Query handles dependent queries cleanly: `enabled: !!userId && !!filters` skips queries until prerequisites are met. SWR has it via conditional fetcher but it's less explicit.
debate.evidence: Dependent query patterns are common; explicit > implicit.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Both libraries support dependent queries; the syntax differs. For new teams, SWR's conditional fetcher (return null to skip) reads more naturally than React Query's enabled flag.
debate.evidence: Subjective; depends on team mental model.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For mutations + cache invalidation across the 30 widgets when the filter changes, React Query's queryKey + invalidateQueries is the cleanest pattern. SWR's mutate has similar capability but the API is less ergonomic at scale.
debate.evidence: invalidateQueries({queryKey: ['dashboard']}) invalidates all queries whose key starts with 'dashboard'. Pattern-based invalidation.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: At scale (30+ widgets, cross-widget invalidations), React Query's hierarchy wins. SWR is fine for 5-10 query app. 30 widgets passes the inflection point.
debate.evidence: Scale-dependent tooling decisions.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Agreed. React Query for this dashboard scale. placeholderData: prev for stale-during-refetch behavior.
debate.evidence: Same default behavior via one config.

where_they_agree: Devtools quality matters for dashboard-scale apps
Hierarchical cache invalidation is needed for 30 widgets
One config option achieves SWR-like default in React Query
where_they_disagree: Whether SWR's simpler API is worth the smaller feature set
Where the scale inflection point is between SWR and React Query
context_dependent_answer: Dashboard with 30 widgets + global filters: React Query with placeholderData. Simple app (5-10 queries): SWR. Mutation-heavy app: React Query (better mutation/optimistic patterns). Static data app (rarely refetches): SWR is sufficient.
decision_framework: debate-079: judge the data fetching strategy debate in this scenario: A dashboard refreshes 30 widgets when a global filter changes. Options: stale-while-revalidate with SWR keeping stale data visible during refetch, vs React Query with manual placeh. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors compare default behaviors. Senior insight: defaults are configurable; the question is what costs you pay over time. React Query's bigger API costs more upfront but its devtools save more later. SWR's simplicity costs less upfront but limits debugging. Pick based on which side of that trade-off your team is. Also: 'stale-while-revalidate' is the right pattern for almost all dashboard reads - this isn't an SWR-specific concept, just SWR's name.

scenario: A design system needs to support runtime theme switching: light/dark, plus per-customer brand colors (white-label). Options: CSS variables for everything, Tailwind v4 with @theme inversion, or runtime CSS-in-JS for the brand colors.
context: 500 customer brands × 2 modes (light/dark) = 1000 theme variants. Static build per brand is impractical. Theme variables update at runtime when admin changes brand colors.
alex_initial_position: CSS variables at the root, JS to set them at runtime. Zero CSS-in-JS overhead, browser-native theme switching, themes change instantly. Tailwind with @theme can read these variables.
sam_initial_position: Tailwind v4 with theme inversion + CSS variables. Best of both: Tailwind for utility ergonomics, CSS variables for runtime themability. CSS-in-JS for brand colors is unnecessary overhead.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: We agree on CSS variables + Tailwind. The disagreement was about runtime CSS-in-JS for brand colors. That's the thing to push back on - generating CSS at runtime per brand is unnecessary when you can just set --color-primary: oklch(...) on document.documentElement.
debate.evidence: Setting one CSS variable updates all dependent styles instantly. Generating a stylesheet at runtime is wasteful.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Strong agree on CSS variables as the mechanism. CSS-in-JS for theming is solving a problem CSS variables already solve, with worse runtime perf.
debate.evidence: CSS variables: O(1) update. CSS-in-JS: O(n stylesheets) recomputation.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: For 500 brands × 2 modes, store the variable values in a database/cache, fetch on user login, set on document root. Zero stylesheet generation. Brand colors are 5-10 variables; the rest of the design system is static.
debate.evidence: 5-10 brand variables × 1000 variants = 5000-10000 values, ~50KB JSON. Trivial to fetch and apply.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Edge case: if a brand needs to override more than colors (font, spacing scale, border radii), the variable count grows. Some brands want different border radius (rounded vs sharp). At 50+ variables per brand, the variable-soup becomes hard to reason about.
debate.evidence: White-label customers often have opinions beyond colors.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: 50+ variables is still manageable as CSS vars. The reasoning challenge is documentation, not technology. Document the variable contract, expose a theme schema for brand admins to configure.
debate.evidence: Variable count isn't the problem; schema discoverability is.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Granted. The harder case: dark mode + brand color requires inversion logic. Brand sets --primary in light mode; dark mode needs an automatic shift (e.g., increase lightness for visibility). CSS variables don't compute; you need JS to derive dark-mode variants.
debate.evidence: OKLCH color math at runtime: small JS, but JS nonetheless.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Compute the dark-mode shift in JS once at theme-load, store both variants, set the active set when mode switches. ~20 lines of color math, runs once per brand load.
debate.evidence: OKLCH-based shifts are deterministic; precompute.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: That works. Net architecture: CSS variables for theme tokens, JS for runtime theme assembly (light/dark variants from brand colors), Tailwind reading the variables. No runtime CSS-in-JS needed.
debate.evidence: Lean stack with variables doing the work.

where_they_agree: CSS variables are the right mechanism for runtime theming
Tailwind v4 reading CSS variables is the right styling layer
Runtime CSS-in-JS is wasteful for this case
Color math (light → dark) in JS once at theme-load
where_they_disagree: Where variable count starts to become unmanageable (Sam: ~50; Alex: documentation problem not tech)
context_dependent_answer: 500-brand white-label: CSS variables + Tailwind @theme + JS theme-loader that computes light/dark from brand primary. For 5 brands: static CSS files per brand, no runtime computation. For single-brand app: hardcoded in @theme. For SaaS with infinite customization: schema-driven theme tokens with admin UI.

strengths: 500 brands × 2 modes = configuration data, not generated CSS
weaknesses: Variable count grows with theme depth
OKLCH math for dark mode requires JS

decision_framework: debate-080: judge the styling approach wars debate in this scenario: A design system needs to support runtime theme switching: light/dark, plus per-customer brand colors (white-label). Options: CSS variables for everything, Tailwind v4 with @theme i. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors look at theming as 'how to swap stylesheets'. Senior insight: themes are values, not stylesheets. CSS variables let you parameterize the rendering. Once styles are parameterized, theming is just setting values. Generating CSS at runtime is solving the wrong problem - we don't need new CSS, we need new VALUES. Also: white-label brand colors need automatic light/dark derivation. Brand admins set 1 color, the system derives the full palette.

scenario: A marketing site needs Lottie animations (designer-created vector animations) on the hero, plus subtle scroll-driven micro-animations. Team debates Lottie vs Rive vs CSS-only.
context: Marketing team has Lottie expertise (designers export from After Effects). Mobile-first audience, conversion-critical hero. Hero animation is 250KB JSON (Lottie format).
alex_initial_position: Rive. 100x smaller files than Lottie, runtime is faster, designed for the web. The 250KB Lottie file is the kill condition - that's blocking first paint on 4G.
sam_initial_position: Lottie. Designers know it. Switching to Rive requires retooling. The 250KB is real but solvable: lazy-load the animation, show a static poster image until JS hydrates. Pay the byte cost AFTER the hero is visible.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Rive's tooling is mature now - Figma plugin, dedicated Rive editor, exports state-based animations natively. Designers learn Rive in a few weeks for what saves 200KB+ per animation forever.
debate.evidence: Rive vs Lottie file sizes: typically 5-20KB Rive vs 100-500KB Lottie for equivalent animations.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Designer ramp-up is a team velocity hit. 'A few weeks' across 4 designers = significant calendar time. Lazy-loading Lottie with a poster doesn't have that cost.
debate.evidence: Lazy-load + poster: 2 days of engineering work. Rive migration: weeks of design retooling.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Lazy-load + poster works for the hero but doesn't help below-the-fold animations. If you have 10 Lottie files at 250KB each, that's 2.5MB of total page weight even with lazy loading. Mobile users on 4G or worse are penalized.
debate.evidence: Page weight across all assets matters for data plans, not just first paint.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Below-the-fold should be different animations - smaller, simpler. Not all animations need vector complexity. CSS for micro-animations, Lottie for the hero only.
debate.evidence: Right tool per role: hero = high-quality animation; micro-animations = CSS.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Even for the hero, Rive's quality is equivalent to Lottie. The 'Lottie quality is needed' argument is dated.
debate.evidence: Rive supports complex vector animation with timeline + state machine.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Equivalent quality, granted. The remaining argument is designer tooling familiarity. For a team where designers create Lottie content, switching is high-friction for marginal user benefit (250KB hero is solvable via lazy-load).
debate.evidence: Friction vs benefit calc favors status quo.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Compromise: keep Lottie for designer-authored complex animations; use Rive for new animations going forward; CSS for micro. The transition is gradual. New hires learn Rive.
debate.evidence: Gradual transition reduces friction.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Pragmatic. Same designers can stay on Lottie for existing work; learn Rive for new. Tooling overlap is small enough.
debate.evidence: Phased adoption.

where_they_agree: Heavy vector animations need a runtime
where_they_disagree: How serious 250KB+ per animation is on mobile
Whether designer tooling friction outweighs perf wins
context_dependent_answer: Mobile-first marketing with hero + many micro: Rive for new, Lottie kept for legacy, CSS for micro. Internal tools: Lottie is fine (no perf budget). Native apps via React Native: Lottie's mobile SDKs are more mature than Rive. CSS-only: if your animations are within CSS's expressive range (transforms, opacity, simple keyframes), skip both libraries.

strengths: Poster ensures first paint isn't blocked
weaknesses: Network-dependent UX (poster lingers on slow networks)

decision_framework: debate-081: judge the animation library decisions debate in this scenario: A marketing site needs Lottie animations (designer-created vector animations) on the hero, plus subtle scroll-driven micro-animations. Team debates Lottie vs Rive vs CSS-only.. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors pick by output quality. Senior insight: animation library choice is a designer-tooling decision as much as a runtime decision. The designer fluency tax is real. Picking the 'better tool' that designers can't use efficiently is worse than picking the slower tool that designers ship fast. Also: animation file size is a one-time cost; designer velocity is recurring. Trade those off explicitly.

scenario: Team is debating snapshot testing for the component library. Existing setup has 800 snapshot tests across 80 components. Snapshots have caused: false PR failures from minor changes, snapshot churn that nobody reviews, and 'just accept all' habits.
context: Component library used by 5 product teams. PRs to the lib take 2x longer due to snapshot reviews. 'Update snapshots' has become the default response to test failures.
alex_initial_position: Delete snapshot tests. Replace with: (1) Visual regression (Chromatic/Percy) for actual visual changes, (2) RTL behavior tests for interaction logic. Snapshots are testing 'did the output text change' which is neither visual nor behavioral.
sam_initial_position: Keep snapshots, fix the workflow. Snapshots ARE useful for catching unintended DOM structure changes (e.g., accidental wrapper divs that break customer CSS). The problem is review discipline, not the tool.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: DOM structure changes that 'break customer CSS' is a non-issue if the lib has a documented public API (CSS classes, data-attributes for selectors). If customers select by random DOM structure, they're using an undocumented API. Snapshots enforce the wrong contract.
debate.evidence: Library API stability is about documented selectors, not DOM stability.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Documented selectors are the contract in principle. In practice, customers DO select by DOM structure (parent > child > nth-child). Snapshot failures surface those breakage points before customer reports.
debate.evidence: Customer support tickets often cite 'we used :nth-child(3) on the Card component'. Snapshots catch this.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: That's solving customers' bad practices by constraining our internal flexibility. The right answer is: document the API, add CSS classes for selection, add a stability badge. Customers who selected by nth-child weren't following the contract. Snapshots punish us for their bad practices.
debate.evidence: Internal flexibility > customer-side bad practice protection.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For internal libs, maybe. For component libs used by 5 product teams in the same company, those teams are us. We're not 'enforcing internal flexibility' against external customers; we're protecting our own teams from churn.
debate.evidence: Internal customer base needs different treatment.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: For internal customer base, replace snapshots with explicit tests for the public API surface. 'Card renders with role=region and aria-label from prop' is a behavior test that catches the structure changes that matter without flagging cosmetic ones.
debate.evidence: Behavior-driven explicit tests vs implicit structure snapshots.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Granted that explicit behavior tests are better than snapshots for the things that matter. The remaining value of snapshots: catching unintended changes you didn't think to test for. A wrapper div added accidentally during refactor doesn't fail behavior tests but does fail snapshots.
debate.evidence: Snapshots catch 'unknown unknowns' that behavior tests miss.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: That's also a property of visual regression (Chromatic). A wrapper div changes the visual output (layout shift). Visual regression catches it with explicit visual diff review. Snapshots catch it as a text diff that no one reviews carefully.
debate.evidence: Visual diffs are more reviewable than text diffs of HTML.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Visual regression is more reviewable but slower and costs money (Chromatic). For the cost, valuable. For a 5-product-team internal lib it's likely worth it.
debate.evidence: Chromatic at scale: $200-500/month for component library coverage.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Net: delete snapshots, add Chromatic for visual, keep RTL behavior tests for interaction. Total cost: Chromatic subscription + migration effort. Win: less PR churn, better signal.
debate.evidence: Phased: delete snapshots over 2 sprints, add Chromatic, write behavior tests for the high-value cases.

where_they_agree: Snapshot review fatigue produces 'accept all' anti-pattern
Visual regression catches unintended visual changes better
Explicit behavior tests catch interaction bugs better
Snapshots are a worse tool for both visual and behavioral signals
where_they_disagree: Whether snapshots have value as a 'safety net for unknown unknowns'
Whether visual regression cost is justified
context_dependent_answer: Component lib (5+ teams): delete snapshots, add Chromatic, write behavior tests. Single-product app: skip snapshots, behavior tests sufficient. Open-source library: Chromatic for paid tier; for free: explicit behavior tests + visual regression on key screens via Playwright + image diff. Don't keep snapshots unless someone is reading them carefully every PR.
decision_framework: debate-082: judge the testing philosophy debate in this scenario: Team is debating snapshot testing for the component library. Existing setup has 800 snapshot tests across 80 components. Snapshots have caused: false PR failures from minor changes. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors love snapshots because they're easy to write. Senior insight: tests have signal-to-noise ratios. A test that fires on cosmetic changes 90% of the time and behavioral changes 10% of the time will be ignored 90% of the time, including the 10% when it matters. The fix isn't writing more tests; it's writing tests with high signal. Visual regression has higher signal because diffs are visual. Behavior tests have higher signal because they assert intent. Snapshots have low signal because they assert 'this string didn't change'.

scenario: A team debates using generic constraints vs conditional types for a `<Field>` component that infers value type from input type ('text' → string, 'number' → number, 'date' → Date).
context: Component library, 30+ field types planned. Type errors should be helpful for consumers. Current implementation has `any` due to type complexity.
alex_initial_position: Generic constraints + discriminated union of props. Each input type is a discriminated branch with explicit value type. No conditional types needed; the compiler infers from the discriminator.
sam_initial_position: Conditional types mapping inputType → valueType. More elegant, less boilerplate. The 30 field types become a 30-key map type, not 30 prop branches.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Conditional types break tooling - go-to-definition jumps to the conditional type definition instead of the relevant branch. Hover shows the conditional, not the resolved type. Discriminated unions show the active branch directly.
debate.evidence: VSCode tooling: discriminated unions show resolved type on hover; conditional types show the conditional expression.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Hover on the FieldProps<'text'> shows the resolved type after the conditional. The tooling has improved a lot. Conditional types' verbosity at the definition site is offset by their conciseness at the call site.
debate.evidence: TypeScript 5.x conditional resolution displays final types on hover.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Final types display on hover, granted. The problem: error messages. When a consumer passes the wrong value type, the error from a conditional type is 'Type X is not assignable to type Y' where Y is the conditional expression. Error from discriminated union is 'Type X is not assignable to type string (for type='text')'.
debate.evidence: Error message quality directly correlates with discriminated unions vs conditional types.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Error quality is the strong argument. For a public component library where consumers will hit type errors, discriminated unions produce better DX.
debate.evidence: DX of error messages > DX of definition-site brevity.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: For 30+ field types, discriminated unions get long but they can be generated. Use a const object as source of truth, derive both runtime config and types from it.
debate.evidence: const FIELD_TYPES = { text: { valueType: '' as string }, number: { valueType: 0 } } as const; → derive type.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Derive-from-const is a strong pattern. Bridges the gap: source of truth in one place, types and runtime config from one declaration.
debate.evidence: Single source of truth pattern.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Combine: discriminated union for the public API + const-derived types from a fields registry. Adding a new field type is one const entry; type errors are clean.
debate.evidence: Best of both: clean error messages + maintainable additions.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Agreed. The 'conditional types are elegant' framing is right for internal types but wrong for public API. Public APIs need readable error messages.
debate.evidence: Public vs internal type design has different optima.

where_they_agree: Discriminated unions produce better error messages than conditional types
Const-derived types are the source-of-truth pattern
Public API ergonomics matter more than definition-site elegance
Adding a field type should be one config change
where_they_disagree: Whether conditional types' definition-site brevity is worth their error-message cost (resolved: no for public API)
context_dependent_answer: Public component library: discriminated unions + const-derived types. Internal types (used only in library implementation): conditional types are fine for brevity. Schema-validation libraries (Zod, Valibot): conditional types because the schemas express the conditional logic. Static analysis tools: lean on conditional types because tooling is the consumer.

weaknesses: Type-specific props (min/max for number) harder to express
Error messages occasionally show conditional expressions
Tooling jumps to the conditional first

decision_framework: debate-083: judge the typescript strictness debate in this scenario: A team debates using generic constraints vs conditional types for a `<Field>` component that infers value type from input type ('text' → string, 'number' → number, 'date' → Date).. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors aim for the most concise type. Senior insight: types are read 10x more than written. Concise definitions can hurt readability and error messages. The 'most elegant' type might be the worst DX. Also: TypeScript types are a UX surface. Error messages are part of the API. Designing types is partially designing error messages.

scenario: Whether to ship 'use memo' (React Compiler) into a mature codebase where 60% of components have manual useMemo/useCallback with eslint-react-hooks/exhaustive-deps passing. Performance budget is fine; team feels the manual memoization is noise.
context: 15-engineer team, 3-year-old codebase, ~250 components, modern React 19.
alex_initial_position: Adopt the Compiler aggressively, delete manual memoization. The noise is real - PRs are full of useMemo wrapping derived values that don't need it. Compiler does this correctly with zero source noise.
sam_initial_position: Adopt the Compiler but DON'T delete manual memoization at first. Run both in parallel for 2 sprints, profile, then delete what the Compiler has handled.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Running both adds verification overhead. The Compiler's output includes the same caching behavior. There's no situation where both manual+Compiler is needed; one or the other.
debate.evidence: Compiler emits useMemoCache hooks equivalent to manual useMemo.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Parallel running isn't 'use both for the same value' - it's keeping manual in place for 2 sprints to verify Compiler enables for those files. Some files won't be Compiler-eligible (rule violations) and we need to know which before deleting manual code.
debate.evidence: Compiler silently skips components with rule violations. Need a way to detect which components are Compiler-managed.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: ESLint plugin reports rule violations. After enabling lint, fix violations, then delete manual memo in bulk. No parallel period needed.
debate.evidence: react-compiler ESLint plugin surfaces violations explicitly.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Fair. Sequence: enable lint, fix violations, enable Compiler, profile, delete manual memo. Parallel-run was overcautious.
debate.evidence: Sequential migration with lint as the gate.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: That's the plan. The hold-out: keep manual memoization for components with custom areEqual or measured perf criticality. Those stay; the rest go.
debate.evidence: Compiler doesn't handle custom areEqual; opt-out is required.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Agreed. Plus: document the rule. 'New components don't add manual memoization; Compiler handles it.' Otherwise the codebase will drift back.
debate.evidence: Documentation as ratchet.

where_they_agree: Manual memo deletion is bulk after Compiler enabled
where_they_disagree: How long to parallel-run (Sam initially: 2 sprints; Alex: 0 with lint as gate)
context_dependent_answer: Mature codebase: enable lint, fix violations, enable Compiler, delete manual memo in bulk. New codebase: enable Compiler from day 1, never write manual memo unless lint+profile shows necessity. Library code: manual memo remains the API contract; don't rely on Compiler in consumer apps.

weaknesses: Some 'useMemo(() => new Date(), [])' are intentional stability

decision_framework: debate-084: judge the performance vs dx tradeoffs debate in this scenario: Whether to ship 'use memo' (React Compiler) into a mature codebase where 60% of components have manual useMemo/useCallback with eslint-react-hooks/exhaustive-deps passing. Performa. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors view manual memoization as 'optimization'. Senior insight: in the React Compiler era, manual memo is mostly noise. The compiler's analysis is more correct than human guesses about referential stability. Keeping manual memo 'just in case' is paying maintenance for a worse version of what the compiler does.

scenario: Migration from a 5-year-old Webpack monorepo (200KLOC, 15 internal packages) to a faster bundler. Options: stay on Webpack, migrate to Vite, migrate to Rspack.
context: Cold dev start: 45s. HMR: 3-5s. CI build: 12 minutes. Team wants <5s HMR and <5 minute CI.
alex_initial_position: Rspack. Drop-in replacement for Webpack (same config shape), Rust-fast, no config rewrite. Vite migration requires rewriting the build config which is months of work.
sam_initial_position: Vite. Rspack is a faster Webpack but it inherits Webpack's mental model. Vite's native ESM dev is a fundamentally different speed class. The migration investment pays back for years.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Vite's native ESM dev is amazing for new projects. For a 5-year-old codebase with mixed CJS deps, custom Webpack loaders, and 5000+ files, the Vite migration requires changing all of that. Rspack uses the same loaders we have.
debate.evidence: Vite migration from Webpack at past role: 4 months for similar codebase. Rspack migration would have been weeks.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: 4 months is real cost. But the resulting dev experience is significantly better. Vite's HMR is <100ms; Rspack improves over Webpack but doesn't match native ESM.
debate.evidence: Vite HMR benchmarks: 50-100ms. Rspack: 200-500ms (faster than Webpack but not Vite-fast).

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: 4 months of engineering time × 15 engineers = 60 engineer-months opportunity cost. Even at 200ms HMR, Rspack is fast enough for daily work. Vite's incremental win isn't worth the migration cost.
debate.evidence: Opportunity cost matters.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: 60 engineer-months is high; the migration is owned by 2-3 engineers, not the whole team. Real cost is ~6 engineer-months, not 60.
debate.evidence: Migration ownership is bounded.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Granted on the cost framing. Where Rspack wins: zero config changes means zero risk of regressions in CI builds, in production builds, in source maps. Build tool migrations are notorious for subtle behavior differences.
debate.evidence: Vite + Webpack: different module resolution, different CSS handling, different env var injection. Each is a potential bug.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Vite's behavior differences from Webpack are real but mature. There's a stable migration path with documented gotchas.
debate.evidence: Vite docs include Webpack migration guide.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Strong point. The decision: Rspack for risk-averse migration with immediate (smaller) wins; Vite for ambitious migration with larger wins later.
debate.evidence: Risk vs reward framing.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For 5-year-old monorepo with active product development: Rspack first (capture quick wins), evaluate Vite migration in 12 months when momentum allows.
debate.evidence: Phased: Rspack now, Vite later.

where_they_agree: Webpack-as-is is too slow for the team
Build tool migrations carry risk of subtle regressions
where_they_disagree: Whether Vite's larger speed win justifies its migration cost
context_dependent_answer: 5-year-old Webpack monorepo: Rspack now, evaluate Vite in 12 months. New project: Vite from day 1. Webpack monorepo with simple config (no custom loaders): Vite migration is more feasible. Webpack monorepo with custom loaders + plugins: Rspack reduces migration cost.

weaknesses: Some Webpack plugins not yet ported

decision_framework: debate-085: judge the build tool choices debate in this scenario: Migration from a 5-year-old Webpack monorepo (200KLOC, 15 internal packages) to a faster bundler. Options: stay on Webpack, migrate to Vite, migrate to Rspack.. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors compare benchmarks. Senior insight: build tool migration cost is real and depends on what your config does. A 'drop-in' upgrade (Rspack from Webpack) is hours-days. A 'paradigm shift' migration (Webpack to Vite) is weeks-months. The benchmark difference matters only if the migration is cheap relative to the gain.

scenario: A 4-team product company is debating: build internal components from Radix primitives, fork shadcn (copy-paste), or adopt Headless UI with custom styling.
context: ~40 engineers, 50+ components needed across teams, 18-month horizon. Designers want a clear system with high custom control.
alex_initial_position: Headless UI. Simpler than Radix (less primitives, fewer edge cases), enough accessibility, lower cognitive overhead for engineers.
sam_initial_position: Radix primitives + custom styling. Headless UI has gaps (no menu submenu, no advanced positioning); Radix is more complete and the design system can lean on its accessibility primitives long-term.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Radix has 30+ primitives with many sub-components each. Headless UI has 8 components, well-documented, easier to learn. For a 4-team adoption, easier-to-learn wins.
debate.evidence: Headless UI docs: 1 page per component. Radix: per-primitive with many sub-pages.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Headless UI's smaller surface is also its limitation. When you need submenu (Radix DropdownMenu.SubTrigger), or a virtualized listbox, or floating UI positioning - Headless UI doesn't have it. You end up building from scratch alongside it.
debate.evidence: Headless UI gaps: no submenu, no nav menu, simpler positioning, fewer ARIA patterns.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: For 80% of the components a product needs (button, dialog, dropdown, popover, tabs), Headless UI is complete. The 20% niche cases (submenu, navmenu) are rare.
debate.evidence: Component usage histogram: top 8 components account for ~85% of usage.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Even within the 80%, Radix's edge case handling is better. Dialog's auto-focus behavior, Popover's collision detection, DropdownMenu's keyboard navigation - all handled more thoroughly in Radix.
debate.evidence: Edge case audit: Radix handles 27 documented accessibility patterns per component on average; Headless UI 12-15.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Edge case fidelity is real. For a public product (millions of users including AT users), it matters. For internal tooling, the difference is smaller.
debate.evidence: AT user prevalence varies by audience.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For product, even small AT user prevalence justifies the better primitive. 4 teams building product: Radix wins.
debate.evidence: Compliance + product-quality angle.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Concede Radix for product. Where I'd push: don't fork shadcn. Copy patterns from shadcn (its component compositions on Radix are well-designed), but maintain your own components. Shadcn's distribution model (copy-paste from CLI) is fine for solo devs; for a 4-team org, you want a shared internal package.
debate.evidence: Shared package vs per-team copies: governance and consistency.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Strong agree. Radix primitives + internal shared package built with shadcn-style compositions as reference. Single owner team for the package; teams contribute via PR.
debate.evidence: Shared package + reference patterns from shadcn.

where_they_agree: Radix primitives over Headless UI for product
Internal shared package over per-team copies
Shadcn as reference, not literal distribution
Designated owner team for the lib
where_they_disagree: How significant the edge-case handling difference is for internal vs product audiences
context_dependent_answer: 4-team product company: Radix primitives + internal shared package + shadcn-style reference. Internal tooling: Headless UI for simpler stack. Mobile-first product: react-aria (better mobile gesture handling). Open source library: Radix primitives + standalone exports.
decision_framework: debate-086: judge the component library strategy debate in this scenario: A 4-team product company is debating: build internal components from Radix primitives, fork shadcn (copy-paste), or adopt Headless UI with custom styling.. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors pick by feature lists. Senior insight: component primitive choice is downstream of accessibility-needs and component count. For internal admin (low AT prevalence, ~20 components), Headless UI is enough. For product (high AT prevalence, 50+ components), Radix's edge case handling pays off. Also: 'distribution model' (npm package vs copy-paste) matters for governance. 4 teams need shared package; solo dev wants copy-paste.

scenario: A real-time collaboration app needs to handle errors during WebSocket sync (peer disconnects, conflict resolution failures, server errors). Question: per-document boundary that recovers via re-sync vs page-level boundary that prompts user to refresh.
context: Multi-user editor, 5-50 concurrent users per document. WebSocket can fail; reconnection logic exists but corruption is possible. Saving local edits is critical.
alex_initial_position: Per-document boundary with auto-recovery: clear local sync state, re-fetch from server, re-establish WebSocket. User sees a brief 'reconnecting' indicator. No data loss because local edits were committed.
sam_initial_position: Page-level boundary with user-initiated refresh. Auto-recovery is dangerous: if the local state is corrupted, re-syncing may overwrite valid local edits. Better to fail loud and let user decide.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: 'Fail loud' for transient errors (peer disconnect, network blip) is terrible UX. The user is mid-edit, the screen says 'Something went wrong, please refresh' - they lose flow state. Auto-recovery for the 95% of errors that are transient is better.
debate.evidence: Connection blips are far more common than corruption. Optimizing for the common case wins.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Auto-recovery is fine for known-transient errors. The error boundary catches UNKNOWN errors - render errors from corrupted state. Re-syncing on those can compound the corruption. For unknown errors, user-initiated refresh is safer.
debate.evidence: Render-time errors from corrupted state: re-syncing doesn't fix the corruption.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Distinguish error types. Network errors (caught at fetch layer) trigger auto-recovery. Render errors (caught at error boundary) prompt user. Two paths.
debate.evidence: Error classification by source: network vs render.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Agreed. Error boundary fires on render errors - prompt user. Network layer handles transient with auto-retry. Different mechanisms for different error classes.
debate.evidence: Layered error handling matches error source.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: For the user prompt on render errors: include 'Save local copy' option. The user's recent edits may be in local IndexedDB; offer to export them as a recovery file before refresh.
debate.evidence: Data preservation as user-controlled.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Strong agree. Recovery file export gives users agency. Also: in the error report telemetry, include the document state hash so we can debug corruption patterns.
debate.evidence: Telemetry-driven debugging.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Granular boundaries: one per document panel. If one panel's editor crashes, others stay alive. Document-level boundary that covers the whole editing surface but doesn't blow up the navigation/sidebar.
debate.evidence: Granularity preserves UX even on errors.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Document panel boundaries with telemetry, user-prompted recovery with local-export option, network errors auto-recovered. Net architecture is clear.
debate.evidence: Layered, classified error handling.

where_they_disagree: Initial framing: auto-recover all vs user-initiated all (resolved by classification)
context_dependent_answer: Real-time collab: network auto-retry + per-document render boundary + local-export recovery. Single-user app: simpler page-level boundary acceptable. Offline-first app: more aggressive auto-recovery (try local, retry sync, last resort prompt). Mission-critical (medical, financial): always prompt; never auto-recover state that may be corrupted.
decision_framework: debate-087: judge the error boundary strategy debate in this scenario: A real-time collaboration app needs to handle errors during WebSocket sync (peer disconnects, conflict resolution failures, server errors). Question: per-document boundary that rec. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors put one ErrorBoundary at root. Senior insight: error handling is a UX design exercise. The error class (network, render, business logic) dictates the recovery model. Auto-recovery for some, user-prompt for others, telemetry for all. Also: critical data (user edits) must be preserved before any reset. Most error boundaries lose data because they blow away local state.

scenario: An app's bundle is 600KB; cold load is 4s on 4G. Team considers route-level splitting only vs route + heavy-component splitting (chart libs, rich editors) vs feature-flag-driven splitting.
context: 24 routes, ~10 'heavy' components used in 5 routes. Feature flag system exists.
alex_initial_position: Route + heavy-component splitting. Most bundle weight is in heavy components (charts, editors) used in some routes. Splitting those at component level (loaded on interaction) gives biggest wins.
sam_initial_position: Route-level only, evaluate component-level only after profiling. Aggressive component splitting adds suspense flashes during navigation, hurts perceived perf. Profile before splitting.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Heavy components have known costs (recharts ~120KB, tiptap ~150KB). You don't need a profiler to know they shouldn't be in initial bundle. Default-split heavy known deps.
debate.evidence: Bundle analyzer shows top 5 deps in 10 minutes.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Default-split heavy deps if they're behind interaction (modal open, click). Above-the-fold heavy components stay in route bundle - splitting creates suspense flash on first paint.
debate.evidence: Above-the-fold splits move bundle bytes to suspense fallbacks, net negative.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Agreed - above-the-fold stays. Below-the-fold or interaction-triggered: split. Idle prefetch the likely-next chunks.
debate.evidence: Idle prefetch via requestIdleCallback hides latency.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Idle prefetch requires manifest of likely-next routes. That's work, but worth it for the 5 'hot path' routes (probably 80% of clicks).
debate.evidence: Prefetch ROI is concentrated on hot paths.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Feature-flag-driven splits I'd push back on. They couple bundle graph to flag state, creating 'flag flip = slow load' anti-pattern.
debate.evidence: Flag flips become slow because chunk wasn't prefetched.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Agreed on feature-flag splits. Reserve for 'feature X is 10% of bundle and 5% of users see it' - rare.
debate.evidence: Debate-088 evidence: sam grounds this claim in the stated Moment.js bundle-size scenario and the row's requirement to distinguish real bundle regressions from harmless date-formatting concerns.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Net plan: route-level + heavy-component splitting (interaction-triggered only) + idle prefetch hot routes. Feature-flag splits later if needed.
debate.evidence: Layered, measurable.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Agreed.
debate.evidence: Debate-088 evidence: sam grounds this claim in the stated Moment.js bundle-size scenario and the row's requirement to distinguish real bundle regressions from harmless date-formatting concerns.

where_they_agree: Idle prefetch is required for non-trivial splits
Heavy components benefit from interaction-triggered splits
where_they_disagree: How aggressive to be by default (Alex: default-split heavy; Sam: profile-first)
context_dependent_answer: Standard SaaS: route + interaction-triggered heavy-component + idle prefetch hot routes. Marketing site: aggressive route-level only. Internal admin: minimal splitting; simpler builds.

weaknesses: Suspense fallback visible on slow networks

decision_framework: debate-088: judge the code splitting philosophy debate in this scenario: An app's bundle is 600KB; cold load is 4s on 4G. Team considers route-level splitting only vs route + heavy-component splitting (chart libs, rich editors) vs feature-flag-driven sp. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors split everything. Senior insight: splitting redistributes latency, doesn't remove it. Splits without prefetch make first interaction slower. The 'savings' show in bundle size but cost in interaction time. Measure both.

scenario: A custom focus management system for a SPA: should it use focus-trap-react (battle-tested but adds 8KB) or build minimal focus trap (~50 LOC, no dep) for a single modal use case?
context: App has 1 modal type, used on 5 pages. Team values bundle size.
alex_initial_position: Build it. focus-trap-react handles many edge cases we don't have. 50 LOC is maintainable.
sam_initial_position: Use focus-trap-react. Edge cases include: iframes inside modal, shadow DOM, dynamic content. Our 'one modal type' will grow.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: For one modal type with static content, edge cases don't apply. 8KB for unused complexity is wasteful.
debate.evidence: YAGNI for unused edge case handling.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: 'One modal type' today. In 18 months, modals will have nested popovers, embedded videos (iframes), dynamic content. Then the 50 LOC needs rewrites.
debate.evidence: Modal complexity grows over time.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Rewrite when needed. Until then, 50 LOC is sufficient.
debate.evidence: Just-in-time complexity.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Rewrites mid-product are painful. Adopting focus-trap-react upfront amortizes the complexity.
debate.evidence: Refactor cost > library cost for complex problems.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Trade-off: 8KB now or rewrite later. For a small team, deferring complexity wins. For a large team, paying upfront wins.
debate.evidence: Team scale dictates.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For accessibility specifically: missing edge cases means SR users have broken experiences. 'Defer complexity' is fine for perf; for a11y it's negligence.
debate.evidence: A11y bugs harm users; perf bugs slow them.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Conceding for a11y. focus-trap-react it is.
debate.evidence: A11y > 8KB.

where_they_agree: A11y bugs are worse than perf bugs
'Build it' was wrong for a11y-critical features
where_they_disagree: How significant 8KB is in this context
context_dependent_answer: focus-trap-react for any non-trivial modal. Custom only for trivial cases (single static modal, no nested interactive content).
decision_framework: debate-089: judge the accessibility tradeoffs debate in this scenario: A custom focus management system for a SPA: should it use focus-trap-react (battle-tested but adds 8KB) or build minimal focus trap (~50 LOC, no dep) for a single modal use case?. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors weigh 'I could write this'. Senior insight: focus management has many edge cases. Building it yourself misses them. Library cost is small; missing edge cases cost real users.

scenario: A 4-product company is debating a frontend BFF (Backend-for-Frontend) pattern: each frontend team owns a BFF that aggregates backend services, vs frontends calling backend services directly with a GraphQL gateway, vs frontends using a shared API client library.
context: 4 frontend teams, 12 backend services. Frontends currently call services directly, leading to N+1 requests, version coupling.
alex_initial_position: BFF per frontend team. Each BFF tailored to that team's needs. Independent deploys, scope of responsibility clear.
sam_initial_position: GraphQL gateway. One layer, multiple frontends consume it. No per-team BFFs proliferating. Schema federation handles service boundaries.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: GraphQL gateway is one team's problem (gateway team) for 4 frontends. Becomes a bottleneck - any schema change needs gateway team coordination.
debate.evidence: Central gateway = central coordination.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: BFF per team = 4 BFFs to maintain, 4 deploy pipelines, 4 sets of auth handling. Duplication.
debate.evidence: BFF proliferation = maintenance overhead.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Duplication is fine if it isolates teams. Each BFF is small (few endpoints), owned by that team. No cross-team coordination.
debate.evidence: Independence > DRY at team scale.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: 4 BFFs × small ≠ no overhead. Auth, logging, rate limiting, telemetry - replicated. Cross-cutting concerns proliferate.
debate.evidence: Cross-cutting concerns need centralization.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Cross-cutting via shared library + BFF template. Auth, logging come from shared - per-BFF code is just endpoints.
debate.evidence: Shared infra + BFF templates.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Then we're describing GraphQL gateway with team-specific resolvers. Same thing, different name.
debate.evidence: BFF-with-shared-infra ≈ gateway-with-resolvers.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Architecturally similar; ownership different. BFF: each team owns deploy. Gateway: gateway team owns deploy.
debate.evidence: Ownership model is the actual difference.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For 4 frontend teams, BFFs are reasonable. For 40, would need a gateway. Scale dictates.
debate.evidence: Scale-dependent architectural choice.

where_they_agree: Ownership model is the actual axis
where_they_disagree: At what scale ownership-distribution (BFF) vs central (gateway) wins
context_dependent_answer: 4 frontend teams: BFF per team with shared infra. 40+ teams: GraphQL gateway. Single product, single backend: direct API calls (no BFF needed).
decision_framework: debate-090: judge the frontend architecture patterns debate in this scenario: A 4-product company is debating a frontend BFF (Backend-for-Frontend) pattern: each frontend team owns a BFF that aggregates backend services, vs frontends calling backend services. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors view BFF as 'just another backend'. Senior insight: BFF is about ownership boundary. The technical pattern matters less than 'who deploys, who debugs, who owns latency'. Pick based on team structure, not feature checklist.

scenario: A note-taking app with extensive offline support and CRDT (Yjs) for collaboration. Team debates: keep CRDT state in Zustand (one store, Yjs as source), or use Yjs directly via React bindings (y-react), or expose state via React Query treating CRDT as a queryable cache.
context: Single editor, multiple panes per note (title, body, tags, comments). Offline editing common.
alex_initial_position: Y-react direct bindings. Yjs is the source of truth; React subscribes to specific Y.Maps/Y.Arrays. Components only re-render when their specific sub-tree changes. No middleware.
sam_initial_position: Zustand as React-facing layer with Yjs underneath. Subscribe to Yjs in Zustand, push updates as Zustand state. Components consume via familiar Zustand selectors.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Adding Zustand creates two sources of truth - Yjs and Zustand. They can drift if anyone forgets to wire an update. Direct Yjs bindings have one source.
debate.evidence: Dual-source bugs are common when CRDTs are wrapped.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Zustand mirrors Yjs - Yjs is canonical. Components use Zustand for ergonomics. The 'two sources' is a mirror, not divergence.
debate.evidence: Mirror pattern: one canonical, others derived.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Mirrors drift in practice. New code path adds a Yjs update without re-mirror to Zustand → component shows stale.
debate.evidence: Mirror discipline fails over time.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Automation: useSyncExternalStore wraps Yjs subscription, pushes to Zustand on every Yjs change. One subscription, automatic sync.
debate.evidence: Automatic mirror via useSyncExternalStore.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: At that point you've reimplemented y-react. Use y-react directly.
debate.evidence: Wrap reinvention.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: y-react's API is awkward for composed state (note has title + body + tags + comments, each a Yjs structure). Zustand wraps them in one ergonomic API.
debate.evidence: y-react has per-structure hooks; Zustand can compose.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Wrap y-react with a custom useNote hook that subscribes to all sub-structures of a note. Ergonomic without Zustand.
debate.evidence: Custom hook for composition.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Custom hook is fine if it's well-designed. For a small team, Zustand is a known pattern; custom hook is bespoke. Risk-reward: known pattern wins.
debate.evidence: Convention > invention for small teams.

where_they_disagree: Whether Zustand wrapper is worth the indirection
context_dependent_answer: Note app with Yjs: y-react + custom composition hooks. Zustand wrap only if team has Zustand muscle memory. React Query for CRDT is wrong - cache invalidation doesn't fit CRDT semantics.
decision_framework: debate-091: judge the state management architecture debate in this scenario: A note-taking app with extensive offline support and CRDT (Yjs) for collaboration. Team debates: keep CRDT state in Zustand (one store, Yjs as source), or use Yjs directly via Reac. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors put CRDT state in their familiar state library. Senior insight: CRDT semantics don't match cache or store models. CRDT is event-sourced; store models are state-at-a-point. Trying to model CRDT as a store creates impedance mismatch. Embrace CRDT primitives directly.

scenario: Building a Toast notification API. Options: imperative API (toast.success('Saved')), declarative (<Toast type='success' message='Saved' />), or hybrid (declarative with imperative trigger via Zustand).
context: Used in 100+ places across app. Need queueing, priorities, dismiss-on-route-change.
alex_initial_position: Imperative. The call site is one line: toast.success('Saved'). Declarative would require state management at every call site.
sam_initial_position: Hybrid: declarative renderer + imperative trigger. Component state in Zustand, trigger functions exposed. Best of both.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Pure imperative is simpler. Call toast.success - the library manages everything. No Zustand boilerplate, no provider, no state management.
debate.evidence: react-hot-toast: pure imperative, minimal setup.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Pure imperative hides state from React's reconciler. Test setup is harder (mock the imperative API), SSR is awkward (imperative calls during render).
debate.evidence: Test mocks for imperative APIs are fragile.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: react-hot-toast works in tests fine. SSR isn't an issue if you don't call toast during render (which is bad pattern anyway).
debate.evidence: Test discipline + render discipline.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Hybrid solves both: state in Zustand (testable, SSR-safe), trigger functions exposed (ergonomic call site).
debate.evidence: useToastStore.getState().add({...}) - functional + ergonomic.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Hybrid adds an explicit store to maintain. For 90% of use cases that's complexity for nothing.
debate.evidence: Complexity for unused flexibility.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For 10% of cases (queue management, route-change dismiss), hybrid is much cleaner. Subscribe to route changes, dismiss queue.
debate.evidence: Power users benefit.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: react-hot-toast supports route-change dismiss via its API. Imperative doesn't preclude advanced features.
debate.evidence: react-hot-toast has the API.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Granted react-hot-toast covers most cases. For first-party design system: hybrid for testability + Zustand familiarity. For adopting library: react-hot-toast.
debate.evidence: Build vs adopt.

where_they_disagree: Whether to build hybrid or adopt imperative library
context_dependent_answer: Adopt react-hot-toast for most teams. Build hybrid for first-party design system needing custom integration with auth, error boundaries, etc.
decision_framework: debate-092: judge the component composition patterns debate in this scenario: Building a Toast notification API. Options: imperative API (toast.success('Saved')), declarative (<Toast type='success' message='Saved' />), or hybrid (declarative with imperative . Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors pick based on call-site syntax. Senior insight: imperative-vs-declarative is a state-ownership question. Imperative hides state in the library; hybrid shares it; declarative pushes it to consumers. The right answer depends on testability and integration needs, not syntax preference.

scenario: Prefetching strategy for a multi-page SPA: prefetch on hover (likely click), on viewport (link in view), on idle (any time after load), or on demand (only on click).
context: Marketing site with high bounce rate. Conversion depends on page load speed after first click.
