alex_initial_position: On-hover prefetch. Highest signal of intent. Minimal wasted prefetches.
sam_initial_position: On-idle prefetch for top 5 hot routes + on-hover for everything else. Idle prefetch warms cache for the most likely paths regardless of hover.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Idle prefetch wastes bandwidth for users who don't click. Mobile data plans matter.
debate.evidence: Idle prefetch can waste 100-500KB per user who bounces.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: saveData check - skip idle prefetch on metered connections. Network Information API surfaces this.
debate.evidence: Network-aware prefetch.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: saveData has spotty browser support, doesn't reflect real conditions. Even good networks have variable cost.
debate.evidence: saveData is set by user, often not.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For top 5 hot routes, the wasted prefetch is small absolute bytes. Win on conversion (faster click-to-render) likely outweighs.
debate.evidence: Conversion uplift from prefetch: measurable.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Hover prefetch captures same conversion uplift with minimal waste. ~200ms between hover and click is enough to fetch most assets.
debate.evidence: Hover-to-click: ~200ms median.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Mobile has no hover. Tap is the first signal, by which time it's too late for prefetch.
debate.evidence: Mobile UX: tap-only.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: On mobile, viewport-triggered prefetch for in-view links. Mobile-specific strategy.
debate.evidence: Touch device detection + viewport prefetch.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Layered: desktop hover, mobile viewport, idle for top routes on all. Mobile-aware strategy.
debate.evidence: Layered prefetch.

where_they_agree: Top hot routes get more aggressive prefetch
where_they_disagree: Whether idle prefetch for hot routes is worth bandwidth
context_dependent_answer: Marketing site with high bounce: hover + viewport + selective idle for hot routes. SaaS app (logged-in): hover sufficient. PWA / offline-capable: aggressive idle for offline cache.
decision_framework: debate-093: judge the data fetching strategy debate in this scenario: Prefetching strategy for a multi-page SPA: prefetch on hover (likely click), on viewport (link in view), on idle (any time after load), or on demand (only on click).. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors prefetch everything. Senior insight: prefetching is a bandwidth-vs-latency trade. Wasted prefetches cost users on metered data. Smart prefetch is signal-driven (hover, viewport, idle for hot routes). Also: 'hover' doesn't exist on mobile - layered strategy needed.

scenario: Atomic CSS framework choice: Tailwind v4 vs UnoCSS vs Panda CSS. All zero-runtime, all utility-first. Which?
context: New project, 3-engineer team. Want fast build, flexible token system.
alex_initial_position: Tailwind v4. Largest ecosystem, well-known patterns, IDE tooling mature.
sam_initial_position: UnoCSS. Faster builds, more flexible, smaller. Tailwind v4 caught up but UnoCSS still leads.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Tailwind v4 is the rewrite that closed the gap with UnoCSS. Native CSS variables, faster compile, modern config.
debate.evidence: Tailwind v4 benchmarks vs UnoCSS: comparable now.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: UnoCSS is more flexible - presets for any utility flavor (Tailwind, Mini, Wind), shortcuts, attributify mode. Tailwind is opinionated.
debate.evidence: UnoCSS preset system.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Opinionation is a feature for 3-engineer teams. Less to configure, faster onboarding.
debate.evidence: Tailwind: zero config gets you 90% there.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: 3 engineers means choices are made quickly anyway. UnoCSS's flexibility doesn't slow you down.
debate.evidence: Configuration speed for small teams.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: IDE tooling: Tailwind IntelliSense is best-in-class. UnoCSS extension is okay but Tailwind's is years more mature.
debate.evidence: Tailwind IntelliSense: autocomplete, hover for resolved CSS, color preview.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: IDE tooling matters. For autocomplete-driven development, Tailwind wins.
debate.evidence: Dev experience > marginal compile speed.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: For a new 3-engineer project: Tailwind v4. Pick the well-known tool.
debate.evidence: Tooling + community + ecosystem.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Agreed. UnoCSS for special cases (need attributify, want preset diversity). Tailwind default.
debate.evidence: Tailwind as default.

where_they_agree: IDE tooling matters for daily DX
Small teams benefit from opinionated defaults
where_they_disagree: How much UnoCSS's flexibility matters for a 3-engineer team
context_dependent_answer: 3-engineer new project: Tailwind v4. Multi-framework (Vue + React + Svelte): UnoCSS (cross-framework). Migrating from Windi: UnoCSS direct successor. Panda CSS: when you want TypeScript-native styles (like Vanilla Extract with utility ergonomics).
decision_framework: debate-094: judge the styling approach wars debate in this scenario: Atomic CSS framework choice: Tailwind v4 vs UnoCSS vs Panda CSS. All zero-runtime, all utility-first. Which?. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors compare benchmarks. Senior insight: utility CSS frameworks are commoditized; pick the one with best ecosystem fit. Tailwind's IntelliSense alone is worth picking it for daily DX. Marginal compile differences are invisible day to day.

scenario: Spring physics vs easing curves for UI transitions (modal open, drawer slide, card hover). Framer Motion supports both. Which feels more natural?
context: Product targets premium feel. Mobile-first. Animation perf budget tight.
alex_initial_position: Easing curves (cubic-bezier). Designable, predictable, repeatable. Springs feel inconsistent across cases.
sam_initial_position: Springs. Native-feel animations track user gestures naturally. iOS feels native because of spring physics.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Springs are great for gesture-driven (drag, scroll). For state transitions (modal open), curves give designers exact control over feel.
debate.evidence: Designers can hand-tune cubic-bezier; springs require physics tuning (mass, stiffness, damping).

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Spring config maps to physical metaphors that designers can intuit. 'stiffness 300' = snappy; 'damping 30' = bounce. Once learned, springs are designable.
debate.evidence: motion.dev spring config presets approximate iOS/Android system feels.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Curves have fewer parameters - 4 numbers. Springs have 3-5 numbers but the interaction is non-linear. Designer A's tuning doesn't intuit for designer B.
debate.evidence: Spring tuning consistency: hard.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For gesture-tracked animation, curves can't continue from interrupted state. Spring handles 'user releases mid-drag' naturally - momentum + decay.
debate.evidence: Drag release: spring continues from current velocity.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Granted - gestures need springs. State transitions (modal, drawer that's just slide-in/out) don't have momentum concerns - curves are sufficient.
debate.evidence: State vs gesture differentiation.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For state transitions, curves give exact control. Use curves for state, springs for gestures. Layered.
debate.evidence: Different optima for different animation types.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Agreed. Standardize: cubic-bezier presets for state transitions (modal: in '0.0 0.0 0.2 1', out '0.4 0.0 1 1'), springs for gesture animations.
debate.evidence: Standardized presets.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Plus: respect prefers-reduced-motion. For users with vestibular sensitivities, replace springs with simple opacity fades.
debate.evidence: A11y respect.

where_they_disagree: How designable spring physics is in practice
context_dependent_answer: Premium feel: curves for state, springs for gestures, prefers-reduced-motion override. Internal admin: simple CSS transitions, skip animation complexity. iOS-like: springs everywhere (matches platform).
decision_framework: debate-095: judge the animation library decisions debate in this scenario: Spring physics vs easing curves for UI transitions (modal open, drawer slide, card hover). Framer Motion supports both. Which feels more natural?. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors apply one animation style everywhere. Senior insight: animations have different goals - state transitions communicate change, gestures track input. They need different physics. Also: prefers-reduced-motion is not optional - vestibular disorders are real, affect ~3% of users.

scenario: E2E test data strategy: factories that generate test data via API, fixtures (pre-built JSON), or real API with seeded staging data.
context: 50 E2E tests, 10 minutes runtime. Test data setup is 30% of runtime.
alex_initial_position: Fixtures. Deterministic, fast, no API dependency for setup. Tests run in isolation.
sam_initial_position: Factories. Fixtures drift from real schema. Factories generate via API, ensuring schema compatibility.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Factory setup is slow (API roundtrips for each test). Fixtures are loaded in milliseconds.
debate.evidence: Factory setup: 200-500ms per test. Fixture load: <10ms.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Schema drift on fixtures is the silent killer. Backend changes field name, fixtures still use old name, tests pass against backend but represent old schema. Drift undetected.
debate.evidence: Drift bugs: subtle, hard to diagnose.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Fixtures generated from schema (TypeScript types) prevent drift. Generate fixtures from same source of truth as backend.
debate.evidence: Type-derived fixtures: codegen from OpenAPI/schema.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Schema-typed fixtures are fixtures with type safety - still don't exercise real validation. Backend's runtime validation rejects fixtures that pass type check.
debate.evidence: Type-correct ≠ runtime-valid.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Use real API for setup once per test suite, then snapshot results as fixtures. Hybrid: real setup, fast load.
debate.evidence: Snapshot fixtures combine both.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Snapshot fixtures still drift if backend's validation changes. Worth it for the speed but with periodic regeneration.
debate.evidence: Snapshots need periodic refresh.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Regenerate on schedule (weekly CI job) or on schema change detection. Drift contained.
debate.evidence: Automated regeneration.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Agreed. Snapshot fixtures + scheduled regeneration + fallback to factories for edge cases.
debate.evidence: Layered.

where_they_disagree: Initial framing: fixtures-as-source vs factories-as-source (resolved by snapshot pattern)
context_dependent_answer: Mature E2E suite: snapshot fixtures with weekly regeneration + factories for edge cases. New suite: factories first, switch to snapshots when patterns emerge. Schema-stable backend: fixtures fine. Volatile schema: factories or short snapshot refresh.
decision_framework: debate-096: judge the testing philosophy debate in this scenario: E2E test data strategy: factories that generate test data via API, fixtures (pre-built JSON), or real API with seeded staging data.. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors use fixtures because tests are fast; ignore drift. Senior insight: tests' value depends on representing reality. Fixtures that drift represent stale reality. Periodic refresh is the bridge. Tests don't have to choose between fast and real - snapshot pattern gets both.

scenario: Image strategy debate: Next.js component (with built-in optimization) vs manual responsive, vs IPX/imgproxy with custom .
context: Image-heavy app, mobile-first, mid-tier device users common.
alex_initial_position: Manual . Zero JS, fully browser-native, no runtime cost. Next has hydration overhead per image.
sam_initial_position: Next <Image>. Auto blur placeholder, auto sizing, auto lazy-load. DX wins; perf overhead is minimal.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Next <Image> per-image JS adds up. 50 images per page × ~200 bytes hydration data = 10KB.
debate.evidence: Hydration data for Image component is small but non-zero.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: 10KB is negligible. The win - auto-optimized sources, blur placeholder, CLS prevention - is enormous DX.
debate.evidence: CLS from images is the #1 CLS source. <Image> fixes it automatically.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: CLS can be fixed with manual width/height on . Aspect-ratio CSS makes it trivial. Doesn't need a component.
debate.evidence: width/height attributes prevent CLS for known-aspect images.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Manual width/height requires knowing dimensions at template time. For user-uploaded content, that's an extra query. <Image> handles it.
debate.evidence: User-uploaded images: dimensions known at upload, used at render.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Store dimensions in DB at upload time, pass to . Same effect, no JS.
debate.evidence: Backend stores; frontend renders.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Granted - backend dimensions + manual srcset works. <Image> automates it. Net: same UX, less code with <Image>.
debate.evidence: Automation reduces template boilerplate.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: For 50 images per page, the <Image> abstraction's hydration overhead matters. Measure on mid-tier Android.
debate.evidence: Lighthouse on image-heavy pages with <Image>: TBT impact visible.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For 5-10 images: fine. For 50+: consider . Scale-dependent.
debate.evidence: Image count matters.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Agreed. <Image> for 1-20 images per page; manual for image galleries with 50+.
debate.evidence: Threshold.

where_they_agree: Image optimization (WebP, AVIF) handled by infra layer (Next, CDN)
where_they_disagree: Where the threshold is between <Image> and manual
context_dependent_answer: for typical product pages (<20 images). Manual for image-heavy galleries (50+ images). CDN-based (imgproxy, Cloudinary) for transformations.
decision_framework: debate-097: judge the performance vs dx tradeoffs debate in this scenario: Image strategy debate: Next.js component (with built-in optimization) vs manual responsive, vs IPX/imgproxy with custom .. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors compare features. Senior insight: image strategy is a CLS-and-bandwidth question. The right approach prevents CLS, sends right-sized images, lazy-loads below-fold. Whether via or is implementation detail.

scenario: Catching async errors. ErrorBoundary doesn't catch them. Options: try-catch in async functions + setState, React 19's enhanced ErrorBoundary, or window.onerror/onunhandledrejection.
context: App with many async operations (fetches, animations, web worker calls).
alex_initial_position: try-catch + setState. Explicit, local handling. Where the error occurs is where it's handled.
sam_initial_position: Library-level wrapper that converts async errors to React Component errors. React Query and similar do this - they throw on render if the query errored.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: try-catch is the platform mechanism. Catching at the source, handling locally is correct.
debate.evidence: Native error handling pattern.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Local try-catch scatters error handling. Same error class (network error, validation) needs same treatment everywhere. Library converts to component-level error.
debate.evidence: Centralized handling via boundary.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: For network errors, agreed - React Query throws on render, ErrorBoundary catches. For non-fetching async (Web Worker messages, animation callbacks), try-catch is the path.
debate.evidence: Different async sources need different handling.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For Web Workers, wrap in a Promise + React Query (or similar) to get error-throwing-on-render behavior.
debate.evidence: Promise wrapping for non-fetching async.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Wrapping every async source in a Promise just to fit React Query's model is overkill. try-catch is fine.
debate.evidence: Wrap overhead.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For consistent error handling UX (toast, dialog, retry), wrapping is worth it. Otherwise every async error site needs its own UI.
debate.evidence: Consistency via centralized.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Centralize the UI (toast helper, error dialog) but call from try-catch sites. Doesn't require throwing-on-render.
debate.evidence: Centralized UI, decentralized handling.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: That works. window.onunhandledrejection as last-resort to catch missed try-catches. ErrorBoundary for render errors. try-catch for explicit async.
debate.evidence: Layered.

where_they_agree: Library wrappers (React Query) help for fetching
where_they_disagree: Whether to wrap all async in promise-throwing patterns
context_dependent_answer: Fetching: React Query/SWR with ErrorBoundary. Other async: try-catch + centralized UI. Last resort: window.onunhandledrejection telemetry.
decision_framework: debate-098: judge the error boundary strategy debate in this scenario: Catching async errors. ErrorBoundary doesn't catch them. Options: try-catch in async functions + setState, React 19's enhanced ErrorBoundary, or window.onerror/onunhandledrejection. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors think ErrorBoundary catches everything. Senior insight: ErrorBoundary catches RENDER errors only. Async errors are caller's responsibility unless wrapped in render-throwing pattern. The 'error boundary doesn't work' bug is misunderstanding scope. Different async sources need different paths.

scenario: Hexagonal architecture for frontend: should domain logic be isolated from React (in pure modules) and consumed via 'application' layer? Or is React-coupled logic fine?
context: 15-engineer team, mature React app, debating refactor priorities.
alex_initial_position: React-coupled is fine. Domain logic that's only ever used in React doesn't benefit from isolation. Pre-mature abstraction.
sam_initial_position: Isolate. Pure domain modules are testable in milliseconds. React-coupled logic requires JSDOM, longer test runs, more mocks.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Pure module abstraction means logic + React are split across files. Reading 'how does pricing work' requires opening two files. Higher friction.
debate.evidence: Cognitive cost of file split.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For non-trivial logic, separation aids testing AND reasoning. 'Pricing logic' should be analyzable independent of UI.
debate.evidence: Test speed gap: 200ms vs 5ms per test.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Test speed matters at scale. For 100 tests: 20s vs 0.5s. Difference is real.
debate.evidence: Test runtime.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: And reasoning aside: domain logic accessed from CLI scripts, server-side, future features. Pure modules are reusable.
debate.evidence: Reuse across contexts.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Most frontend domain logic isn't reused server-side. The 'future reuse' is hypothetical.
debate.evidence: YAGNI.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For domain-rich logic (pricing, inventory, billing math), it's reused. For UI-only (form state, modal state), keep in React.
debate.evidence: Per-logic decision.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Agreed. Isolate domain-rich logic; keep UI-state logic in React. Not 'all hexagonal' or 'no hexagonal'.
debate.evidence: Selective isolation.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Net: pure modules for domain logic (pricing, business rules); React-coupled for UI state (form, modal). Per-feature judgment.
debate.evidence: Selective architecture.

where_they_disagree: Default position: isolate by default (Sam) vs keep coupled by default (Alex), resolved to selective
context_dependent_answer: Selective: pure modules for domain logic with combinatorial complexity (pricing, business rules), React-coupled for UI state. Full hexagonal: only when team has time and clear separation needs.
decision_framework: debate-099: judge the frontend architecture patterns debate in this scenario: Hexagonal architecture for frontend: should domain logic be isolated from React (in pure modules) and consumed via 'application' layer? Or is React-coupled logic fine?. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors put everything in components. Senior insight: components are render+state holders. Logic that doesn't render belongs elsewhere. Domain logic in components mixes concerns and slows testing. Extract logic that's interesting in its own right.

scenario: Mutations + optimistic updates: useOptimistic (React 19) vs React Query mutations vs custom optimistic-update logic.
context: Heavy-mutation app (favorites, likes, draft updates). UX requires instant feedback.
alex_initial_position: useOptimistic. React 19's built-in. No external state lib. Aligned with React's mental model.
sam_initial_position: React Query mutations with onMutate optimistic. Mature pattern, server state managed, retry/error handling built in.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: useOptimistic handles the UI optimistic update; React Query handles the server. They're not competing - layered.
debate.evidence: Use both for different layers.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For most cases, React Query's onMutate handles optimistic UI through query cache update. useOptimistic adds another layer.
debate.evidence: queryClient.setQueryData inside onMutate.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: queryClient.setQueryData mutates the cache. Reverting on error requires onError. useOptimistic does this declaratively.
debate.evidence: useOptimistic is React-native; React Query is imperative.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: React Query's revert is one line: rollback context with previous cache. Declarative enough.
debate.evidence: queryClient.setQueryData(key, prevData) in onError.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: useOptimistic auto-reverts when the action completes (whether success or error). React Query needs explicit rollback.
debate.evidence: useOptimistic semantics: auto-cleanup.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For complex optimistic (multiple updates in transaction), React Query's onMutate gives more control. useOptimistic is per-component, less coordinated.
debate.evidence: Cross-cache mutations need coordination.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Per-component optimistic is fine for component-local UI. Cross-cache concerns use React Query.
debate.evidence: Per-use-case split.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Agreed. useOptimistic for UI-local optimistic; React Query for cross-cache.
debate.evidence: Layered.

where_they_disagree: How often UI-local optimistic is enough
context_dependent_answer: UI-local (favorite toggle, like): useOptimistic. Cross-cache (mutation affecting multiple queries): React Query. Custom logic: only for edge cases where neither fits.
decision_framework: debate-100: judge the data fetching strategy debate in this scenario: Mutations + optimistic updates: useOptimistic (React 19) vs React Query mutations vs custom optimistic-update logic.. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors pick one tool. Senior insight: optimistic UI has two scopes - local (visible only to this component) and cross-cache (other components see). They have different optimal tools. Don't force one into the other.
